longTailKeywords: blood pressure spike spinal cord injury

h1Title: Autonomic Dysreflexia: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Autonomic dysreflexia is a potentially life-threatening syndrome of uncontrolled, paroxysmal hypertension occurring in people with spinal cord injuries at or above the T6 level, triggered by a noxious stimulus below the level of injury. It affects an estimated 50-90% of individuals with cervical or upper thoracic spinal cord injuries, with episodic blood pressure surges that can reach 300/220 mmHg — sufficient to cause hemorrhagic stroke, seizures, or cardiac arrest if untreated. The condition arises because the intact sympathetic outflow below the spinal injury level is cut off from normal supraspinal inhibition, generating an unchecked vasopressor response. Urinary bladder distension is the most common trigger, accounting for 75-85% of episodes. Immediate identification and removal of the offending stimulus is the first-line emergency treatment.

keyStats.prevalence: 50-90% of people with spinal cord injury at or above T6 experience at least one episode; estimated 20,000-30,000 people affected annually in the US alone (NSCISC 2023)
keyStats.demographics: People with cervical (C1-C7) and upper thoracic (T1-T6) spinal cord injuries are exclusively at risk; complete injuries carry higher risk than incomplete injuries
keyStats.avgAge: Spinal cord injury peaks at age 16-30 years (motor vehicle crashes, sport) and over 65 years (falls); autonomic dysreflexia risk begins immediately post-injury in the spinal shock recovery phase
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 250,000-500,000 new spinal cord injuries occur annually worldwide (WHO 2023); each individual with a high-level injury risks hundreds of dysreflexia episodes over a lifetime

definition: Autonomic dysreflexia (ICD-10: G90.4), also called autonomic hyperreflexia, is a clinical syndrome of episodic paroxysmal sympathetic hyperactivity in individuals with spinal cord injuries at or above the sixth thoracic (T6) spinal segment, defined by a sustained rise in systolic blood pressure of 20 mmHg or more above the individual's baseline. Because baseline blood pressure is often low in spinal cord-injured patients (90-100 mmHg systolic), a systolic of 120-130 mmHg may represent a clinically significant dysreflexia episode. The pathophysiological mechanism involves an unchecked spinal reflex arc: nociceptive or non-nociceptive sensory input from below the injury level activates intact sympathetic preganglionic neurons in the intermediolateral cell column, producing massive splanchnic and peripheral vasoconstriction. Normal supraspinal inhibition via reticulospinal pathways cannot reach below the injury because the spinal cord lesion interrupts descending control. Baroreceptors in the carotid sinus and aortic arch detect the hypertension and send inhibitory signals to the vasomotor centre, producing a compensatory bradycardia and vasodilation above the injury level — but the reflex arc below the injury continues firing because the inhibitory signal cannot cross the lesion. The condition is managed by spinal cord injury rehabilitation medicine specialists, emergency physicians, and urologists, with patient and caregiver education forming the cornerstone of safe long-term management.

typesClassification.description: Sudden episode with blood pressure rise of >20 mmHg above baseline, accompanied by classic symptoms (headache, sweating, flushing above and pallor below the injury level). Requires immediate treatment to prevent stroke or cardiac event.

Blood pressure elevation of >20 mmHg above baseline without recognizable symptoms, detected by ambulatory blood pressure monitoring. Occurs frequently during sleep, bowel programs, and sexual activity. May go unrecognized but carries similar stroke risk with repeated episodes.

Persistent or recurrent episodes despite removal of the trigger, requiring pharmacological blood pressure reduction with nifedipine, glyceryl trinitrate, or captopril. May indicate a missed or refractory trigger such as inguinal hernia, spastic bladder not responding to catheter, or intra-abdominal pathology.

primarySymptoms: Sudden pounding or throbbing headache — often the first and most prominent symptom — caused by cerebral vasodilation as the baroreceptor reflex attempts to counter extreme hypertension above the spinal injury level.
Profuse sweating above the level of spinal injury, particularly over the face, neck, and upper chest, while the skin below the injury level may be cold and dry due to vasospasm.
Facial flushing and redness above the level of injury from reflex vasodilation, sometimes with blotchy red patches over the trunk and upper limbs.
Skin pallor, coolness, and piloerection (goosebumps) below the level of injury from sympathetic vasoconstriction and sudomotor overactivity below the lesion.

Bradycardia — paradoxically slowed heart rate (below 60 bpm) in response to the extreme hypertension via vagally-mediated baroreceptor reflex, contrasting with the expected tachycardia of other causes of hypertensive crises.
Nasal congestion and blurred vision from autonomic mucosal vasodilation during an episode.
Anxiety, restlessness, and a sense of impending doom accompanying severe episodes, reflecting both the systemic effects and the patient's learned recognition of the syndrome.

Paroxysmal hypertension with systolic blood pressure commonly exceeding 150-180 mmHg in cervical injury patients — levels that in individuals without spinal cord injury would prompt emergency intervention.
Minimal or absent symptoms in subclinical episodes detected only by ambulatory blood pressure monitoring — silent dysreflexia during cystoscopy, bowel management, or sleep is well documented.
Penile erection (priapism) or clitoral engorgement as part of the autonomic reflex response in some episodes, a recognized precipitant being sexual activity or urological procedures.
earlyWarningSigns: Onset of a throbbing headache in a spinal cord-injured person at or above T6 should be treated as autonomic dysreflexia and blood pressure measured immediately — other diagnoses are considered only after AD is excluded.
Unexplained sweating or flushing in a person with high-level spinal cord injury performing bowel care or bladder management, even without headache.

Bradycardia on cardiac monitoring during urological procedures (cystoscopy, urodynamics) in spinal cord-injured patients — reflex bradycardia during a procedure signals a concurrent blood pressure surge.
emergencySigns: Systolic blood pressure above 150 mmHg in a person with cervical or upper thoracic spinal cord injury, especially with severe headache — requires immediate intervention to prevent hemorrhagic stroke.

Loss of consciousness, seizure, or focal neurological signs during a hypertensive episode — indicates hypertensive encephalopathy or intracerebral hemorrhage requiring emergency neurosurgical assessment.
Acute pulmonary edema or severe chest pain during an autonomic dysreflexia episode — cardiac emergency requiring immediate blood pressure reduction and resuscitation.

Persistent severe hypertension despite removal of all identifiable triggers — suggests an occult trigger such as intra-abdominal pathology, deep vein thrombosis, or pressure ulcer and requires emergency medical evaluation.

causes.description: The single most common trigger, responsible for 75-85% of episodes. Causes include catheter kinking, blocked catheter, urinary retention, urinary tract infection increasing bladder irritability, urodynamic studies, cystoscopy, and involuntary voiding. Regular bladder management eliminates the majority of preventable episodes.

causes.cause: Bowel distension, fecal impaction, or rectal procedures
causes.description: The second most common trigger (13-19% of episodes). Constipation, fecal impaction, digital stimulation, suppository insertion, and colonoscopy all generate nociceptive afferent input through sacral and lumbar roots. Adequate bowel management programs prevent most bowel-related episodes.

causes.cause: Skin pressure, wounds, and nociceptive stimuli
causes.description: Pressure ulcers, ingrown toenails, burns, constrictive clothing or footwear, insect bites, and skin-level injuries all activate pain afferents below the injury level. The person with a complete spinal cord injury may have no conscious awareness of these stimuli, making a systematic skin-to-head inspection essential during any episode.

Sexual activity, ejaculation, labor and delivery, menstruation, genital manipulation, and urological instrumentation are potent triggers. Obstetricians managing spinal cord-injured patients in labor must recognize and treat autonomic dysreflexia, which is present in up to 85% of deliveries in women with T6 or above injuries.

Fractures below the injury level (often painless), heterotopic ossification, spasticity, and overstretching of joints produce afferent input sufficient to trigger dysreflexia. Occult fractures of the lower limbs from falls or transfers are a recognized and often missed trigger.

Extreme temperature changes (hot or cold exposure below the injury level), sunburn, contact with hot surfaces, and environmental temperature extremes all trigger sympathetic discharges in the isolated spinal cord. Impaired thermoregulation in high-level SCI patients amplifies this risk.

riskFactors.factor: Spinal cord injury at or above T6
riskFactors.description: This is a prerequisite for autonomic dysreflexia — the T6 level is critical because it is above the major splanchnic outflow (T5-T12). Cervical injuries carry the highest risk and the most severe episodes.

Complete injuries (ASIA impairment scale A, no motor or sensory preservation) produce more frequent and severe dysreflexia episodes than incomplete injuries because all descending inhibitory signals are blocked.

Irregular catheterization, blocked catheters, and urinary tract infections are the most preventable risk factors. A structured bladder management program with regular clean intermittent catheterization eliminates the majority of episodes.
Infrequent bowel programs (less than every 48 hours), inadequate hydration, and insufficient dietary fiber are key modifiable risk factors for bowel-triggered episodes.
Pressure ulcers in insensate skin areas are silent triggers that generate continuous low-level nociceptive afferent input. Regular skin inspection and pressure-relief positioning are preventable.
UTI in spinal cord-injured patients increases bladder wall irritability and lowers the threshold for dysreflexia even with a non-obstructed catheter. Prompt UTI treatment prevents episode clusters.
Autonomic dysreflexia generally becomes established during the first year post-injury as spinal shock resolves and spinal reflex arcs are re-established; risk persists for the patient's lifetime.

affectedDemographics: People with cervical spinal cord injury: highest risk group, comprising approximately 55% of all new spinal cord injuries. Dysreflexia affects 50-90% of this group.
Individuals with upper thoracic injury (T1-T6): 30-50% prevalence of autonomic dysreflexia; risk decreases as injury level descends toward T6.
Pregnant women with spinal cord injury at or above T6: up to 85% experience dysreflexia during labor and delivery — one of the highest-risk clinical scenarios.
Adolescent athletes: sport-related cervical spinal cord injuries (diving, rugby, gymnastics) represent a significant proportion of young onset cases.
diagnosisOverview: Autonomic dysreflexia is primarily a clinical diagnosis made at the bedside in the context of a known spinal cord injury at or above T6. The critical diagnostic step is measuring blood pressure — a rise of 20 mmHg or more above the individual's baseline confirms the diagnosis. Because people with high-level spinal cord injuries have chronically low baseline pressures (typically 90-100 mmHg systolic), the Consortium for Spinal Cord Medicine defines a threshold systolic of 150 mmHg as requiring immediate pharmacological treatment regardless of symptoms. The primary diagnostic challenge is identifying the trigger: a systematic head-to-toe survey is performed, beginning with the most common causes (bladder then bowel) while blood pressure management proceeds simultaneously. Laboratory tests during an acute episode include urinalysis and urine culture, chest radiograph, and, if no trigger is found, abdominal plain film for bowel obstruction or impaction. ECG documents the compensatory bradycardia and excludes cardiac arrhythmia. Long-term monitoring with ambulatory 24-hour blood pressure recordings identifies subclinical episodes that occur during sleep or routine bladder and bowel care. Urodynamic testing at baseline and during bladder filling (under supervised conditions with blood pressure monitoring) characterizes the underlying bladder dysfunction driving recurrent episodes and guides management.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood pressure measurement (immediate, repeated every 2-5 minutes)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms diagnosis; documents severity; guides pharmacological treatment threshold
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sitting or semi-reclined position with an appropriately-sized cuff. Blood pressure should be measured every 2-5 minutes throughout any suspected episode. A systolic rise of 20 mmHg or more above the individual's known baseline confirms autonomic dysreflexia.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies urinary tract infection or hematuria as a trigger for the current episode and guides antibiotic selection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Catheter specimen or bladder drainage urine sample; dipstick result within minutes, culture results in 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes catheter obstruction, kinked tubing, and overdistension as immediate triggers — the most common and most rapidly reversible causes
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bedside inspection of the catheter and drainage bag by a nurse or physician; takes under 2 minutes and is the first step in any episode management protocol.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies fecal impaction as the trigger when bladder causes have been excluded
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A brief digital rectal exam performed after anesthetic lubricating gel (lidocaine gel) is applied to minimize further stimulation during the examination.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents compensatory bradycardia, excludes arrhythmia (atrial fibrillation, ventricular ectopy) during hypertensive surge
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard ECG taking under 5 minutes; ideally performed once blood pressure is partially controlled to obtain an interpretable tracing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects asymptomatic (subclinical) episodes of autonomic dysreflexia during daily activities, sleep, and bladder programs; guides chronic management intensity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A wrist or upper-arm cuff connected to a recorder worn continuously for 24 hours, measuring blood pressure every 15-30 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Management of an acute autonomic dysreflexia episode follows a standardized algorithm recommended by the Consortium for Spinal Cord Medicine (CSCM) clinical practice guidelines (2021 update). The first priority is to sit the patient upright — gravity partially reduces venous return and lowers blood pressure by 10-20 mmHg. Simultaneously, a systematic search for the trigger proceeds from bladder to bowel to skin. Tight clothing, pressure garments, and footwear are removed. The bladder is decompressed by draining or replacing the urinary catheter; if no catheter is in situ, in-and-out catheterization is performed after instilling lidocaine 2% gel into the urethra for 2 minutes. If blood pressure remains at or above 150 mmHg systolic after these initial measures, pharmacological treatment is initiated. Rapid-onset antihypertensives recommended by CSCM guidelines include: sublingual nifedipine 10 mg (bite-and-swallow or chew), glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) 0.4 mg sublingual spray or 2% topical paste applied above the level of injury, or oral captopril 25 mg. Intravenous labetalol or hydralazine are reserved for severe refractory episodes in hospital settings. Recurrent or frequent episodes are managed by addressing the underlying trigger cause — supervised intermittent catheterization, botulinum toxin A injections into the detrusor for neurogenic overactive bladder, structured bowel programs, and pressure ulcer prevention. Prophylactic management for planned procedures (cystoscopy, bowel care, labor) includes topical anesthetic premedication and a low threshold for treating blood pressure rises.

medicalTreatments.name: Sublingual nifedipine (10 mg, bite-and-swallow)
medicalTreatments.description: Calcium channel blocker producing rapid blood pressure reduction within 5-10 minutes. Recommended as first-line pharmacological treatment in CSCM guidelines when trigger removal alone is insufficient. Available without IV access. Contraindicated in hemodynamically unstable patients or those already hypotensive.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces blood pressure by 20-30 mmHg within 5-10 minutes in the majority of acute episodes; recommended first-line in CSCM 2021 guidelines

medicalTreatments.name: Glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) sublingual 0.4 mg spray or 2% topical paste applied above injury level
medicalTreatments.description: Rapid-onset nitric oxide donor producing venodilation and arterial relaxation. Topical paste formulation allows dose titration and removal if blood pressure falls excessively. Contraindicated in patients who have taken a phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor (sildenafil, tadalafil) within 24-48 hours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Blood pressure reduction of 15-25 mmHg within 2-5 minutes; can be reversed by wiping off paste formulation if over-treatment occurs

medicalTreatments.name: Captopril (25 mg oral or sublingual)
medicalTreatments.description: ACE inhibitor with onset of action 15-30 minutes; useful for less acute presentations or as an alternative when nifedipine and GTN are unavailable. Also used for prevention of recurrent episodes in patients with frequent severe dysreflexia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for mild to moderate episodes; slower onset than nifedipine or GTN; data from small trials specific to autonomic dysreflexia

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin A intradetrusor injection (onabotulinum toxin A 200 units)
medicalTreatments.description: Prevents recurrent bladder-triggered autonomic dysreflexia by chemically denervating overactive detrusor muscle and increasing bladder capacity. Repeated every 6-9 months. Standard of care for neurogenic bladder in high-level spinal cord injury per EAU guidelines.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces autonomic dysreflexia episode frequency by 65-85% in patients with neurogenic overactive bladder in randomized trials (PMID 26631126)

medicalTreatments.name: Alpha-1 adrenergic blockade (prazosin 1-5 mg daily or terazosin 1-5 mg nightly)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for prophylaxis of recurrent episodes where bladder and bowel triggers cannot be fully controlled. Reduces peripheral vascular resistance at sympathetic adrenergic receptors below the injury level. Evidence limited to observational data but widely used in spinal cord injury rehabilitation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces episode frequency and severity in observational cohorts; risk of postural hypotension in already hypotensive SCI patients requires careful dose titration

medicalTreatments.name: Lidocaine 2% gel (intra-urethral instillation 10 mL, 2 min before catheterization or procedures)
medicalTreatments.description: Pre-procedure topical anesthetic blocking urethral and bladder afferent input to prevent procedure-triggered episodes during urological interventions, cystoscopy, or urodynamics. Standard preventive measure in CSCM guidelines.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Documented reduction of procedure-induced blood pressure surges by 40-60% compared with no premedication in controlled urological series

surgicalOptions.name: Sacral anterior root stimulation (Brindley stimulator)
surgicalOptions.description: Implanted neuromodulation device stimulating sacral roots to provide controlled bladder voiding and reduce uncontrolled detrusor contractions. Posterior root rhizotomy performed simultaneously abolishes the afferent limb of the bladder dysreflexia reflex arc. Used for carefully selected patients with complete cervical or thoracic SCI.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 85-90% of implanted patients achieve continent controlled voiding; major reduction in autonomic dysreflexia episodes from bladder triggers in published series

alternativeTreatments.name: Biofeedback and sympathetic nervous system monitoring
alternativeTreatments.description: Wearable blood pressure and heart rate variability monitors allow real-time detection of subclinical dysreflexia during daily activities. Feedback from these devices enables faster trigger identification and earlier intervention, reducing time-averaged blood pressure exposure.

whySeeSpecialist: Any person with a spinal cord injury at or above T6 who experiences recurrent blood pressure episodes, severe headaches, or unexplained sweating should be seen by a spinal cord injury rehabilitation physician. The condition requires systematic evaluation of bladder and bowel management, a trigger audit, and individualized pharmacological prophylaxis planning.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a physiatrist or rehabilitation neurologist specializing in spinal cord injury at a designated SCI rehabilitation centre. For urological triggers, a urologist experienced in neurogenic bladder management is essential. All acute episodes should be treated at emergency departments familiar with spinal cord injury physiology.
hospitalCriteria: Emergency department with spinal cord injury protocols and access to sublingual nifedipine or GTN
Inpatient spinal cord injury rehabilitation unit with specialist nursing for bladder and bowel management
Urology service with cystoscopy capability and intradetrusor botulinum toxin injection program
24-hour blood pressure monitoring capability for subclinical episode characterization
keyFacilities: Regional spinal cord injury centres with multidisciplinary SCI teams
Neurogenic bladder clinics with urodynamics and botulinum toxin programs
Outpatient SCI rehabilitation and bowel management programs
Emergency departments at spinal cord injury centres with established autonomic dysreflexia protocols
preventionStrategies: Maintain a structured clean intermittent catheterization schedule (every 4-6 hours) or ensure indwelling catheter patency to prevent bladder overdistension — the most important preventable trigger.
Complete a bowel management program at regular intervals (every 24-48 hours) with appropriate agents to prevent fecal impaction.
Perform daily skin inspection using adaptive equipment or with caregiver assistance to identify pressure ulcers, ingrown toenails, and other skin stimuli early.
Educate all caregivers, family members, and emergency personnel about autonomic dysreflexia recognition and the emergency treatment protocol — the patient cannot always communicate their symptoms.
Carry an autonomic dysreflexia emergency card or wear a medical alert bracelet identifying the diagnosis and trigger-search protocol.
Pre-medicate with urethral lidocaine gel and monitor blood pressure during all planned urological and colorectal procedures.
lifestyleModifications: Adhere strictly to the prescribed bladder management schedule; do not extend catheterization intervals beyond the prescribed limit even when busy.
Keep a written or electronic log of each episode — date, time, trigger identified, treatment given, blood pressure readings — to identify patterns and guide management reviews.
Ensure adequate fluid intake (1.5-2 L/day) and dietary fiber (25-30 g/day) to prevent bowel constipation.
Check catheter and drainage bag position and patency before any physical activity, transfers, or sexual activity.
Inform all treating clinicians, dentists, and proceduralists about autonomic dysreflexia and the need for blood pressure monitoring during any procedure.
Review the autonomic dysreflexia management protocol annually with the spinal cord injury specialist and update caregivers when personnel change.

recommended: High dietary fiber intake (25-30 g/day from whole grains, vegetables, and legumes) to maintain regular bowel function and prevent constipation-triggered episodes
Adequate fluid intake (1.5-2 L/day) to prevent concentrated urine, UTI, and bladder irritability
Regular meal timing to maintain consistent bowel motility and predictable bowel program scheduling
Adequate potassium intake (fruits, vegetables) to support cardiovascular regulation in a denervated circulation
avoid: Low-fiber or processed foods that worsen constipation and increase bowel-triggered episode risk
Excessive caffeine or alcohol, which can irritate the bladder and disrupt bowel motility in neurogenic dysfunction
Foods causing individual bloating or gas that increases intra-abdominal pressure on the bowel and bladder

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise — including hand cycling, wheelchair propulsion, adapted aquatics, and functional electrical stimulation cycling — reduces cardiovascular risk and improves autonomic regulation over time in people with high-level spinal cord injury. Blood pressure must be monitored before, during, and after exercise sessions. Exercise-induced autonomic dysreflexia (during functional electrical stimulation or intense activity) is a recognized risk; start supervised and ensure bladder is empty before exercise.
dailyManagement: Catheterize or check catheter patency every 4-6 hours as prescribed; never allow the bladder to become overdistended.
Complete bowel care on schedule with the prescribed combination of digital stimulation, suppositories, and positioning.
Inspect skin over bony prominences, in skin folds, and at catheter entry sites every morning and evening.
Keep blood pressure medication, sublingual nifedipine, or GTN spray within reach at all times as prescribed by the spinal cord injury team.
Brief caregivers and household members on episode recognition and the emergency trigger-search protocol.
Attend scheduled urology, rehabilitation, and primary care appointments to monitor bladder function, UTI status, and cardiovascular effects of recurrent hypertension.
prognosis: Autonomic dysreflexia is a lifelong condition for people with spinal cord injury at or above T6 — there is no cure, and episodes recur throughout life. With structured prevention programs addressing bladder, bowel, and skin triggers, episode frequency can be reduced by 60-80%. The risk of life-threatening complications — stroke, intracerebral hemorrhage, seizures, and cardiac death — is substantially reduced by early recognition and prompt blood pressure treatment. Untreated or repeatedly under-treated episodes cause cumulative cerebrovascular damage: 24-hour blood pressure studies show that subclinical autonomic dysreflexia during sleep exposes the brain to hundreds of hypertensive surges annually. Long-term cardiovascular outcomes in spinal cord injury are strongly associated with the quality of autonomic dysreflexia management. Intradetrusor botulinum toxin A is the most effective current intervention for reducing episode frequency from the dominant bladder trigger, with 65-85% episode reduction sustained over 6-9 months per treatment cycle.
recoveryTimeline: Individual episodes resolve within 1-15 minutes of trigger removal and/or pharmacological treatment. Chronic management requires ongoing prevention — no discrete 'recovery' timeline applies. Intradetrusor botulinum toxin effect lasts 6-9 months; prophylactic alpha-blockers require indefinite daily dosing.
complications: Hemorrhagic stroke from untreated hypertensive surges — the leading serious complication; risk is highest in episodes where systolic pressure exceeds 200 mmHg for more than 30 minutes.
Seizures and hypertensive encephalopathy during severe or prolonged episodes — requires immediate blood pressure reduction and neurological assessment.
Retinal hemorrhage and visual loss from acute hypertension during recurrent severe episodes — detected by fundoscopy during follow-up.
Cardiac arrhythmias including atrial fibrillation and ventricular ectopy during the extreme autonomic surge — detected by continuous cardiac monitoring in high-risk patients.
Pulmonary edema from acute hypertensive left ventricular overload during severe episodes — requires urgent diuresis and blood pressure control.

Psychological distress, anxiety, and reduced quality of life from fear of recurrent life-threatening episodes — rates of PTSD-like symptoms exceed 30% in chronic AD populations requiring mental health support.

supportResources.name: Consortium for Spinal Cord Medicine (CSCM)
supportResources.description: Publisher of clinical practice guidelines for autonomic dysreflexia and other SCI-related conditions; guidelines freely available for patients and clinicians.

supportResources.name: Christopher and Dana Reeve Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient-facing resources including emergency wallet cards, caregiver guides, and an AD management protocol for download.

supportResources.name: Spinal Cord Injury BC
supportResources.description: Canadian peer support and clinical resource organization with detailed patient guides on autonomic dysreflexia prevention and treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Spinal Cord Injury Rehabilitation
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Spinal cord injury rehabilitation encompasses the broad management of all sequelae of SCI; autonomic dysreflexia is one specific acute complication occurring during rehabilitation and in chronic SCI. Not all SCI patients develop AD — only those with injuries at or above T6.

confusedWithConditions.name: Multiple Sclerosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MS can cause autonomic dysfunction and episodic blood pressure changes through demyelinating lesions but rarely produces the paroxysmal hypertensive crises characteristic of autonomic dysreflexia. AD requires a complete or near-complete transverse spinal lesion at T6 or above; MS lesions are typically incomplete and multifocal.

confusedWithConditions.name: Central Pain Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Central pain syndrome causes persistent pain from central nervous system lesions, including spinal cord injury, and shares the SCI context with autonomic dysreflexia. However, central pain is a chronic sensory condition; autonomic dysreflexia is an acute episodic cardiovascular emergency. The two can coexist in the same patient.

relatedConditions.name: Spinal Cord Injury Rehabilitation
relatedConditions.relevance: Autonomic dysreflexia is a direct consequence of high-level spinal cord injury; the entire management occurs within the spinal cord injury rehabilitation framework.

relatedConditions.name: Neuropathic Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Neuropathic central pain below or at the level of spinal cord injury frequently coexists and shares the same afferent pathway activation that can trigger autonomic dysreflexia episodes.

relatedConditions.name: Central Pain Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Central pain is a common comorbidity of high-level SCI and shares the same sensory afferent abnormalities that underlie autonomic dysreflexia trigger hypersensitivity.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple Sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: MS can produce high cervical cord lesions with autonomic dysreflexia-like episodes in rare cases; both conditions require understanding of spinal cord physiology and autonomic regulation.

faqs.question: What is autonomic dysreflexia?
faqs.answer: Autonomic dysreflexia is a sudden dangerous rise in blood pressure affecting people with spinal cord injuries at or above the sixth thoracic vertebra (T6). A stimulus below the injury level — most often a full bladder — triggers an uncontrolled sympathetic reflex, causing blood pressure to spike to stroke-level heights because normal brain control cannot reach below the injury.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of autonomic dysreflexia?
faqs.answer: The classic symptoms are a sudden pounding headache, profuse sweating and flushing above the spinal injury level, skin pallor and goosebumps below it, and a slow heart rate despite high blood pressure. Nasal congestion, blurred vision, and anxiety are common. Severe episodes can cause stroke, seizures, or cardiac arrest.

faqs.question: What causes autonomic dysreflexia?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is a full or blocked bladder, responsible for 75-85% of episodes. Fecal impaction, constipation, pressure ulcers, tight clothing, skin injuries, and sexual activity are other common triggers. Any painful or irritating stimulus below the level of spinal cord injury can trigger an episode.

faqs.question: How is autonomic dysreflexia treated?
faqs.answer: Immediately sit the person upright, then systematically remove the trigger: drain the bladder first (drain catheter or insert a new one), then check for fecal impaction. Remove tight clothing and check for skin injuries. If systolic blood pressure stays above 150 mmHg, give sublingual nifedipine 10 mg or glyceryl trinitrate spray. Call emergency services for severe episodes.

faqs.question: Who is at risk of autonomic dysreflexia?
faqs.answer: Only people with spinal cord injuries at or above T6 — including cervical and upper thoracic injuries — are at risk. Complete injuries carry higher risk than incomplete injuries. It affects an estimated 50-90% of people with cervical spinal cord injury at some point in their lives.

faqs.question: Is autonomic dysreflexia dangerous?
faqs.answer: Yes. Blood pressure can reach 300/220 mmHg in severe episodes — high enough to cause hemorrhagic stroke, seizures, pulmonary edema, and cardiac arrest. Prompt recognition and trigger removal within minutes significantly reduces the risk of these complications.

faqs.question: How do you prevent autonomic dysreflexia?
faqs.answer: Prevention centers on managing the most common triggers: regular bladder catheterization every 4-6 hours, a structured bowel program every 24-48 hours, daily skin inspection for pressure ulcers, and prompt treatment of urinary tract infections. Botulinum toxin injections into the bladder muscle reduce bladder-triggered episodes by 65-85%.

faqs.question: Can autonomic dysreflexia cause a stroke?
faqs.answer: Yes. Untreated episodes with systolic blood pressure exceeding 200 mmHg for more than 30 minutes can cause hemorrhagic stroke through cerebral vessel rupture or severe hypertensive encephalopathy. Chronic repeated subclinical episodes also cause cumulative cerebrovascular damage.

faqs.question: What level of spinal cord injury causes autonomic dysreflexia?
faqs.answer: Autonomic dysreflexia occurs exclusively in spinal cord injuries at or above T6, because the T6 level lies above the major sympathetic splanchnic outflow. Cervical injuries carry the highest risk. Injuries below T6 do not produce autonomic dysreflexia because descending inhibitory control remains partially intact.

faqs.question: What medications treat autonomic dysreflexia?
faqs.answer: Sublingual nifedipine 10 mg (bite-and-swallow) is the most commonly recommended first-line drug. Glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) 0.4 mg sublingual spray or 2% paste applied above the injury is an alternative. Captopril 25 mg oral is used for milder episodes. Intravenous labetalol or hydralazine is used for severe refractory episodes in hospital.

faqs.question: Can autonomic dysreflexia happen during pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Up to 85% of women with spinal cord injury at or above T6 experience autonomic dysreflexia during labor and delivery. Uterine contractions act as a potent trigger below the injury level. Obstetricians managing such patients must monitor blood pressure continuously and treat dysreflexia episodes promptly during labor.

faqs.question: What is subclinical autonomic dysreflexia?
faqs.answer: Subclinical autonomic dysreflexia is a blood pressure rise of 20 mmHg or more above baseline without recognized symptoms. It commonly occurs during sleep, bowel programs, and sexual activity. Ambulatory blood pressure monitoring detects these silent episodes, which carry similar stroke risk to symptomatic episodes with repeated occurrence.

faqs.question: Does autonomic dysreflexia cause pain?
faqs.answer: People with complete spinal cord injury do not feel pain below the injury level, but they experience a severe throbbing or pounding headache above it during autonomic dysreflexia. The headache results from cerebrovascular vasodilation driven by the baroreceptor reflex attempting to counter extreme hypertension.

faqs.question: Can botulinum toxin prevent autonomic dysreflexia?
faqs.answer: Yes, for bladder-triggered episodes. Intradetrusor botulinum toxin A (200 units) reduces neurogenic bladder overactivity and bladder-triggered autonomic dysreflexia episodes by 65-85% in randomized trials. Effects last 6-9 months; repeat injections are performed as needed. It is standard of care for neurogenic overactive bladder in high-level SCI.

faqs.question: What should caregivers do during an autonomic dysreflexia episode?
faqs.answer: Sit the person upright immediately, measure blood pressure, and systematically remove triggers starting with the bladder. Drain or replace the urinary catheter, then check for fecal impaction. Remove tight clothing and check skin. Give prescribed emergency medication if blood pressure stays high. Call emergency services if symptoms are severe or blood pressure does not fall.

metaDescription: Autonomic dysreflexia causes dangerous BP spikes in spinal cord injury above T6. Emergency triggers, nifedipine treatment, and prevention guide reviewed.

sources.title: Consortium for Spinal Cord Medicine. 2021. Acute management of autonomic dysreflexia: a clinical practice guideline. Paralyzed Veterans of America, Washington DC.
